input:
# Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare

## Abbreviations

- AI: Artificial Intelligence
- LLM: Large Language Model
- NLP: Natural Language Processing

## Introduction

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming healthcare. Large Language Models (LLM) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) are key enabling technologies.

AI enables faster diagnosis and triage. LLMs can process vast amounts of medical literature and summarise findings. NLP extracts structured insights from unstructured clinical notes.

## Conclusion

AI, LLM, and NLP will continue to shape the future of healthcare delivery.
target_answer: All three abbreviations (AI, LLM, NLP) are defined inline at first use with the pattern 'Full Name (ABBR)' and are listed in the Abbreviations section. No compliance violations should be reported for any of them.

input:
# Climate Change and Economic Growth

## Abbreviations

- CO2: Carbon Dioxide
- IPCC: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change
- GDP: Gross Domestic Product

## Introduction

CO2 emissions continue to rise globally despite international agreements. The IPCC has issued increasingly urgent warnings about rising temperatures. GDP growth must be decoupled from greenhouse gas emissions.

## Methods

CO2 concentration levels were measured using satellite data. IPCC assessment reports from 2014 to 2023 were reviewed. GDP data was sourced from the World Bank open data portal.

## Conclusion

Decoupling CO2 emissions from GDP growth remains the central challenge of climate policy, and IPCC guidance will continue to inform national targets.
target_answer: CO2, IPCC, and GDP are listed in the Abbreviations section but NONE of them have an inline definition at their first occurrence in the body text — they appear bare without the 'Full Name (ABBR)' pattern. All three should be flagged as compliance violations for missing inline definitions at first use.

input:
# Cybersecurity Landscape Report

## Abbreviations

- TLS: Transport Layer Security

## Introduction

Transport Layer Security (TLS) is the foundational protocol for encrypted web communications. Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks have also grown significantly in scale and frequency.

## Threat Analysis

TLS version 1.3 introduced major improvements over prior versions. DDoS attacks increasingly target critical infrastructure and cloud services.

## Conclusion

Organisations must upgrade to TLS 1.3 and deploy DDoS mitigation strategies to maintain a secure posture.
target_answer: TLS has an inline definition at first use ('Transport Layer Security (TLS)') and is listed in the Abbreviations section — compliant. DDoS has an inline definition at first use ('Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)') but is NOT listed in the Abbreviations section — DDoS should be flagged as a compliance violation for missing from the abbreviations section.

input:
# Defense Technology Assessment

## Office of the Under Secretary of War (OUSW)

The OUSW was established in 1941 to coordinate wartime procurement. The OUSW played a pivotal role in managing contracts across all branches of the armed forces.

## Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA)

ARPA was founded in 1958 in response to the Soviet Sputnik launch. ARPA later became DARPA when the 'Defense' prefix was added.

## Conclusion

Both the OUSW and ARPA represent landmark moments in the institutional history of U.S. defense technology.

target_abbreviations.inline_definition: Office of the Under Secretary of War

target_answer: OUSW and ARPA are both defined inside Markdown headings ('## Office of the Under Secretary of War (OUSW)' and '## Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA)'). These heading occurrences should be marked ignored=true because heading titles are not valid inline definition locations. The first body-text occurrence of each abbreviation (e.g., 'The OUSW was established...' and 'ARPA was founded...') carries no inline definition and should therefore be flagged as compliance violations. U.S. is an exempt abbreviation and should be marked ignored=true.

input:
# Research Team Profiles

## Senior Investigators

Dr. Johnson leads the epidemiology division and has published over 80 peer-reviewed articles. Mr. Patel manages the data infrastructure team and oversees all database systems. Ms. Chen coordinates outreach with partner institutions across the U.S.

## Methodology

All protocols were approved by Dr. Johnson and reviewed by Mr. Patel. Data transfers comply with applicable U.S. federal regulations.

## Conclusion

The team, led by Dr. Johnson, has established a strong track record of collaboration across U.S. institutions.
target_answer: Dr., Mr., and Ms. are personal title abbreviations and U.S. is the United States abbreviation — all are exempt abbreviation classes. Every occurrence of these should be marked ignored=true. No compliance violations should be flagged and the abbreviations list should contain only ignored entries.

input:
# Machine Learning in Drug Discovery

## Abbreviations

- ML: Machine Learning
- FDA: Food and Drug Administration
- API: Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient

## Introduction

Machine Learning (ML) is accelerating the drug discovery pipeline. Regulatory submissions to the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) must now account for ML-derived evidence. The Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API) characterisation phase is one area where ML models have proven particularly effective.

## Applications

ML models can screen millions of molecular candidates in hours. FDA guidance documents now address the validation of ML-based diagnostic tools. API purity testing has been automated using ML-driven spectroscopic analysis.

## References

- Smith, J. and Brown, A. (2022). ML Methods for API Synthesis Optimisation. Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences, 111(4), 1200-1215.
- Jones, R. (2023). FDA Regulatory Frameworks for ML in Clinical Trials. Drug Safety, 46(2), 88-102.
target_answer: ML, FDA, and API are all defined inline at first use with the 'Full Name (ABBR)' pattern and are all listed in the Abbreviations section — all three are fully compliant. Occurrences of ML, FDA, and API inside the References section should be marked ignored=true because the References section is exempt from inline-definition and abbreviations-section rules. No compliance violations should be flagged.

input: Dr. Sarah Chen, Ph.D., and Mr. James Okafor, M.A., co-authored a study on low-power wireless systems, demonstrating that a signal of 15 mW transmitted over a distance of 340 cm achieved reliable coverage. Their findings drew on field tests conducted by Col. Marcus Webb during a U.S. Army communications trial. (U) The unclassified summary confirmed the results, while the full technical annex remains marked Secret pending declassification review. (S) IS has confirmed that this information is stored securely. Artificial Intelligence (AI) is an emerging technology.
target_answer: Dr. and Mr. are personal titles, Ph.D. and M.A. are academic degrees, mW and cm are common units of measurement, Col. is a military rank, U.S. is the United States abbreviation, and (U) and (S) are security markings — all are exempt abbreviation classes and should be marked ignored=true. AI has an inline definition at first use ('Artificial Intelligence (AI)') but is not listed in an abbreviations section (none exists). AI should be flagged as a compliance violation for missing from the abbreviations section. No other compliance violations should be flagged.
